pattern: The suspicious set is a reachability closure from one node along directed calls; removal is only safe when no edge crosses in from outside that closure.
hints: First find every method the buggy one can reach, directly or indirectly, by following invocation edges forward - that whole reachable set is "suspicious".
Removal fails if ANY method outside the suspicious set has an edge INTO the suspicious set - it doesn't matter whether suspicious methods call outward.

BFS/DFS forward from k to build the suspicious set. Then scan every invocation [a, b]: if a is outside the set and b is inside it, nothing can be removed - return every method 0..n-1. Otherwise return the methods not in the set.

edge_inputs.description: no invocations at all - only the buggy method itself is suspicious and removable
a self-contained chain rooted at k with nothing calling in from outside
an outside method calls directly into the suspicious chain, blocking any removal
the blocking call targets a method reached only indirectly (two hops from k), not k itself
a cycle entirely within the suspicious set is fine; only outside-in edges matter
minimum size, a single method with no invocations
the suspicious method calls out to a safe method, which is allowed and does not block removal
k is the last valid index

stress.description: a random DAG of invocations over the max-ish node count with a mid-range k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 2000))
    k = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
    m = min(4 * n, 100000)
    seen = set()
    invocations = []
    attempts = 0
    while len(invocations) < m and attempts < m * 3:
        attempts += 1
        a = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        b = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        if a == b or (a, b) in seen:
            continue
        seen.add((a, b))
        invocations.append([a, b])
    return [n, k, invocations]
stress.note: edges may point either direction, exercising both self-contained and blocked scenarios
